nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, clear to slightly turbid streams and rivers with slow to moderate current; typically found clinging to submerged vegetation, rocks, and woody debris in well-oxygenated water with abundant algae and biofilm growth

decorPreferences: established algae and biofilm coverage on glass, rocks, and driftwood
smooth rounded rocks for grazing surfaces
live plants (Java fern, Anubias, Amazon sword — slow-growers accumulate aufwuchs)
open areas alternating with planted zones

diet.notes: Dedicated aufwuchs grazer — feeds primarily on algae, biofilm, diatoms, and microorganisms covering surfaces in a mature tank. Supplement with algae wafers, blanched zucchini/cucumber/spinach, spirulina wafers, and sinking vegetable-based foods. True herbivore: unlike carnivore-leaning Hypancistrus plecos, Otos will not thrive on meaty protein. Must have continuous access to natural biofilm; specimens in new, algae-free tanks commonly starve within weeks.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetras, neon tetras, rummy-nose tetras)
avoidWith: fast, boisterous fish that outcompete for food

summary: A tiny algae-grazing catfish from Brazilian streams, reaching ~4 cm; a true aufwuchs feeder that requires a mature, established tank with abundant natural biofilm. Must be kept in groups of 6+. Wild-caught with high shipping mortality; trade fish sold as “O. affinis” are frequently O. vittatus or O. macrospilus.
careNotes:
The Dwarf Otocinclus is one of the aquarium hobby’s most effective algae grazers, but its reputation as “easy” is misleading. Its single greatest care challenge is diet: Otos are obligate aufwuchs feeders that rely on algae, diatoms, and biofilm on all tank surfaces. A newly set-up, algae-free aquarium will almost certainly starve them within weeks. A tank should be running and cultivating visible algae growth for a minimum of two to three months before Otos are introduced. Once established, supplement with algae wafers, spirulina-based sinking wafers, and blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, romaine lettuce, spinach) several times per week, placing food directly near their grazing areas.

All commercially available Otocinclus are wild-caught; captive breeding at scale has not been achieved. Wild collection causes significant stress, and shipping mortality is notoriously high. Purchase from reputable importers, quarantine new arrivals for two to four weeks, and observe carefully for sunken bellies (a sign of starvation or internal parasites). Acclimate slowly to match tank parameters.

Otos are shoaling fish and must be kept in groups of at least six; solitary or paired specimens are visibly stressed, hide constantly, and decline faster. Water quality must be pristine — as small, wild-caught fish with limited stress reserves, they are sensitive to ammonia, nitrite, and elevated nitrate. Perform regular partial water changes (25–30% weekly). Soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–15) best reflects their native habitat, though they can adapt to neutral water.

Note on taxonomy: the trade name “Otocinclus affinis” is applied loosely across the hobby; most specimens sold are actually O. vittatus or O. macrospilus, with true O. affinis limited to southeastern Brazil. All three species share nearly identical husbandry requirements. The genus includes approximately 20 described species.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been reported but remains rare and difficult to replicate consistently; there are no established commercial breeding operations. Breeding is most likely to occur in a mature, well-planted tank with excellent water quality and regular partial water changes with slightly cooler water (temperature drop of 2–3°C simulating seasonal variation). Spawning follows a pattern similar to corydoras: males pursue females vigorously, and eggs are deposited on plant leaves and glass. Parents do not guard eggs. Fry require algae and infusoria immediately upon hatching. Success reports in the hobby are uncommon, and most trade fish remain wild-caught.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Otocinclus Catfish Care Guide
primary.notes: Primary trade source. Provided care level, mature-tank requirement, diet (algae wafers, blanched vegetables, biofilm dependence), group size (6+), tank size (minimum 10 gallons / ~38 L; 20+ gallons recommended for groups), water parameters, and starvation risk in new tanks. Aquarium Co-Op explicitly flags the mature-tank requirement and high wild-caught mortality.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Otocinclus affinis
additional.notes: Detailed taxonomic and biotope data. Confirmed adult size (3.5–4.5 cm), water parameters (temperature 20–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 18–143 ppm / ~1–8 dH), native range (southeastern Brazil), habitat (clear shallow streams with abundant vegetation and algae), diet (aufwuchs, diatoms, algae), and breeding notes. Also notes the genus-complex identification problem: most trade “O. affinis” are O. vittatus or O. macrospilus.

additional.name: FishBase — Otocinclus affinis
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), adult size (to 4.4 cm SL), native range (coastal drainages of southeastern Brazil), diet (algae/aufwuchs), and temperature range (20–27°C). Confirmed wild-caught only status and restricted natural range.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Otocinclus species guide
additional.notes: Supplementary source for trade identification issues (genus complex, O. vittatus misidentification), wild-caught shipping stress and mortality, minimum group size (6+), established-tank requirement, and supplemental feeding advice. Confirms intermediate care rating due to mature-tank dependency.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Otocinclus: The Perfect Algae Eater?
additional.notes: Cross-referenced diet (true herbivore/aufwuchs, distinct from carnivore-leaning Hypancistrus), starvation risk in sterile new tanks, supplemental vegetable feeding, and captive breeding rarity. Confirmed constantGrazer requirement and the hobbyist-fatal mistake of adding Otos to newly established tanks.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rare; all commercial supply is wild-caught. Reports exist of spawning in mature, well-planted tanks triggered by cool partial water changes. Eggs deposited on plant leaves and glass; fry require algae and infusoria. No established breeding protocol.
